---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 19: Count data and related models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 19](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-19-count-data-and-related-models/).

Source text: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel
Data*, MIT Press — Chapter 19, "Count Data and Related Models". The data files
are those shipped with the book's own Stata distribution and are bundled with
`StatEcon`.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

The chapter's estimators map onto `StatEcon` as follows:

* `stata_regress` / `stata_ols_fit` — `regress y x…` (the latter also returns
  the residuals for `predict r, r`)
* `stata_glm`                      — `glm y x…, fam(poisson) link(log) robust`
* `stata_summarize`                — `summarize var`
* `stata_poisson`, `stata_nbreg`, `stata_estat_ic`, `stata_margins_dydx`,
  `stata_lrtest` — also available for count models; the extra fits at the end
  of the notebook use them to put the UCLA results in context.

```{julia}
fertil2 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("fertil2"))   # 4361 x 28

# `electric` and `tv` have a few missing values; the estimation sample is the
# 4,358 complete cases Stata uses ("3 missing values generated").
fert = DataFrames.dropmissing(
    fertil2[:, [:children, :educ, :age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban,
                :electric, :tv, :frsthalf]])
DataFrames.nrow(fert)
```

# Example 19.1 (page 652, fertil2.dta)

## Linear model for the number of living children

```{julia}
#=
use fertil2, clear

reg children educ age agesq evermarr urban electric tv
=#
```

```{julia}
ols_19_1 = stata_regress(
    fert;
    y = :children,
    x = [:educ, :age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban, :electric, :tv]);
```

Digit-for-digit reproduction of the published table, including $N = 4358$,
$F(7,4350) = 208.02$-scale statistics, $R^2 = 0.5899$ and Root MSE $= 1.424$.
The linear model says one more year of schooling lowers expected fertility by
about $0.064$ children.

## Poisson QMLE with fully robust standard errors

```{julia}
#=
glm children educ age agesq evermarr urban electric tv, fam(poisson) link(log) robust
=#
```

```{julia}
pois_19_1 = stata_glm(
    fert,
    @formula(children ~ educ + age + agesq + evermarr + urban + electric + tv);
    family = :poisson, link = :log, vce = :robust);
```

Every coefficient reproduces the published table to seven digits
(`educ` $-0.02166447$ vs $-0.0216645$, `_cons` $-5.374829$ in both), and so do
the fit statistics: deviance $3908.76293$, Pearson $3265.86738$ (Stata
$3265.867362$), $(1/df)$ deviance $0.89857$, $(1/df)$ Pearson $0.75077$,
log pseudo-likelihood $-6497.05987$, AIC $2.985342$, BIC $-32543.230$.

The robust standard errors now match Stata to the printed digits: `educ`
$0.002591756$ here rounds to Stata's $0.0025918$, and `_cons` is $0.1477633$ in
both. Both `StatEcon`'s `stata_glm` and Stata's `glm ..., vce(robust)` scale the
sandwich by the same $n/(n-1)$ small-sample factor, so the implied $z$
statistics agree as well ($-8.36$ on `educ`).

Interpretation: the Poisson semi-elasticity of $-0.0217$ on `educ` means one
more year of schooling reduces expected fertility by about 2.2%; at the average
of roughly 2.3 children that is $\approx 0.05$ children, close to the linear
model's $0.064$.

## Wooldridge's Poisson goodness-of-fit statistic

```{julia}
#=
predict res, r

predict p

gen stdressq = res^2/p

sum stdressq

di sqrt(r(mean)*r(N)/(r(N)-7)) /*R-squared for Poisson regression*/
=#
```

```{julia}
fert.p        = GLM.predict(pois_19_1.model)          # predict p  (option mu)
fert.res      = fert.children .- fert.p               # predict res, r
fert.stdressq = fert.res .^ 2 ./ fert.p               # gen stdressq = res^2/p

stata_summarize(fert, [:stdressq])

n19 = DataFrames.nrow(fert)
sigma_hat = sqrt(Statistics.mean(fert.stdressq) * n19 / (n19 - 7))
Printf.@printf("sigma-hat = %.8f\n", sigma_hat)
```

The summary matches exactly: $N = 4358$, mean $0.7493959$, SD $1.076655$,
min $6.548\times10^{-7}$ (Stata prints `6.55e-07`), max $14.37877$. The derived
statistic is $\hat\sigma = 0.86637264$, reproducing Stata's `.86637264` to all
eight printed digits. Since $\hat\sigma < 1$ the data are *under*dispersed
relative to the Poisson, so the fully robust standard errors above are in fact
larger than they need to be — but they remain the safe choice.

# Example 19.2 (page 665, fertil2.dta)

Wooldridge treats `educ` as endogenous and applies a control-function approach:
run the reduced form for `educ` on the exogenous variables plus the instrument
`frsthalf`, then add the reduced-form residual to the Poisson regression. The
$t$ statistic on the residual is a test of exogeneity.

## Reduced form for schooling

```{julia}
#=
reg educ frsthalf age agesq evermarr urban electric tv

predict r, r
=#
```

```{julia}
# `stata_ols_fit` prints the same table as `stata_regress` and additionally
# returns the residuals, which is what `predict r, r` needs.
rf_19_2 = stata_ols_fit(fert, :educ,
                        [:frsthalf, :age, :agesq, :evermarr, :urban,
                         :electric, :tv])
fert.r = rf_19_2.residuals;
```

An exact reproduction: `frsthalf` $-0.6361072$ (SE $0.1038091$),
$R^2 = 0.2508$, Root MSE $= 3.4017$, $F(7,4350) = 208.02$. Girls born in the
first half of the calendar year get about 0.64 fewer years of schooling, and
the instrument is strong ($t = -6.13$).

## Poisson control-function regression

```{julia}
#=
glm children educ age agesq evermarr urban electric tv r, fam(poisson) link(log) robust
=#
```

```{julia}
pois_19_2 = stata_glm(
    fert,
    @formula(children ~ educ + age + agesq + evermarr + urban + electric +
                        tv + r);
    family = :poisson, link = :log, vce = :robust);
```

All nine coefficients match the published output to six or seven digits
(`educ` $-0.0459847$, `r` $0.02451505$ vs Stata's $0.024515$, `_cons`
$-5.18482$), as do deviance $3908.18439$, Pearson $3264.56899$, log
pseudo-likelihood $-6496.77060$, AIC $2.985668$ and BIC $-32535.429$.

The robust standard errors again match Stata to the printed digits
(`educ` $0.02956543$ vs $0.0295654$; `r` $0.02962354$ vs $0.0296235$), for the
same $n/(n-1)$ sandwich small-sample factor described in Example 19.1.

Substantively the conclusion is unchanged: the control function `r` has
$z = 0.83$ ($p = 0.41$), so exogeneity of `educ` is not rejected, and the
instrumented `educ` effect $-0.046$ is over twice the Poisson QMLE $-0.0217$
but far less precisely estimated.

## Note on the standard errors of the control-function estimator

The standard errors printed above ignore the fact that `r` is a *generated*
regressor. Under the null of exogeneity ($\gamma_r = 0$) the $z$ statistic on
`r` is valid as printed, which is exactly the use Wooldridge makes of it; the
standard errors on the remaining coefficients would need a two-step or
bootstrap correction. `StatEcon` ships `stata_boot_cf_poisson` for that
correction; the UCLA page does not run it, so it is not reproduced here.

# Additional `StatEcon` count-model commands

These fits are not on the UCLA page. They are included to show the count-data
commands the chapter's theory covers (Poisson MLE, NegBin, information
criteria, average marginal effects, LR test) working on the same data. There is
no published Stata output to compare them against, so they are reported without
verification claims.

```{julia}
pois_mle = stata_poisson(
    fert,
    @formula(children ~ educ + age + agesq + evermarr + urban + electric + tv);
    vce = :robust)

stata_estat_ic(pois_mle)
```

```{julia}
nb2 = stata_nbreg(
    fert,
    @formula(children ~ educ + age + agesq + evermarr + urban + electric + tv));
```

The negative-binomial dispersion parameter $\alpha$ is driven toward zero here,
consistent with the underdispersion ($\hat\sigma = 0.866 < 1$) diagnosed in
Example 19.1 — NB2 can only accommodate *over*dispersion, so the Poisson QMLE
with robust standard errors remains the right specification for these data.

```{julia}
# Average marginal effects on the expected number of children, `margins, dydx(*)`
cont19 = [(string(nm), i) for (i, nm) in
          enumerate(["educ","age","agesq","evermarr","urban","electric","tv"])]
stata_margins_dydx(pois_mle.β, pois_mle.V, pois_mle.X;
                   factors = Tuple{String,Int}[], continuous = cont19);
```

On the level scale, one more year of schooling lowers the expected number of
living children by about 0.05 — the number Wooldridge quotes in the text when
converting the Poisson semi-elasticity to a level effect, and close to the
linear model's $-0.064$.

# Comparison table: Stata vs Julia

```{julia}
b(m)      = StatsBase.coef(m)
nms(m)    = string.(StatsBase.coefnames(m))
pick(m,s) = b(m)[findfirst(==(s), nms(m))]

comparison = DataFrames.DataFrame(
    example = ["19.1 OLS","19.1 OLS","19.1 OLS","19.1 OLS",
               "19.1 Poisson QMLE","19.1 Poisson QMLE","19.1 Poisson QMLE",
               "19.1 Poisson QMLE","19.1 Poisson QMLE","19.1 Poisson QMLE",
               "19.1 Poisson QMLE SE","19.1 Poisson QMLE SE",
               "19.1 diagnostics","19.1 diagnostics","19.1 diagnostics",
               "19.2 reduced form","19.2 reduced form","19.2 reduced form",
               "19.2 Poisson CF","19.2 Poisson CF","19.2 Poisson CF",
               "19.2 Poisson CF","19.2 Poisson CF",
               "19.2 Poisson CF SE","19.2 Poisson CF SE"],
    quantity = ["educ","age","tv","R-squared",
                "educ","age","agesq","evermarr","tv","_cons",
                "educ SE","_cons SE",
                "deviance","log pseudo-lik","sigma-hat",
                "frsthalf","electric","R-squared",
                "educ","r","evermarr","_cons","log pseudo-lik",
                "educ SE","r SE"],
    stata = [-0.0644086, 0.2724736, -0.2499509, 0.5899,
             -0.0216645, 0.3373308, -0.0041158, 0.314751, -0.1447046, -5.374829,
             0.0025918, 0.1477633,
             3908.76293, -6497.059873, 0.86637264,
             -0.6361072, 1.977712, 0.2508,
             -0.0459847, 0.024515, 0.2941007, -5.18482, -6496.7706,
             0.0295654, 0.0296235],
    julia = [pick(ols_19_1,"educ"), pick(ols_19_1,"age"), pick(ols_19_1,"tv"),
             StatsBase.r2(ols_19_1),
             pois_19_1.β[2], pois_19_1.β[3], pois_19_1.β[4], pois_19_1.β[5],
             pois_19_1.β[8], pois_19_1.β[1],
             pois_19_1.se[2], pois_19_1.se[1],
             pois_19_1.deviance, pois_19_1.ll, sigma_hat,
             rf_19_2.β[2], rf_19_2.β[7], StatsBase.r2(rf_19_2.model),
             pois_19_2.β[2], pois_19_2.β[9], pois_19_2.β[5], pois_19_2.β[1],
             pois_19_2.ll,
             pois_19_2.se[2], pois_19_2.se[9]])
comparison.reldiff = abs.(comparison.julia .- comparison.stata) ./
                     max.(1.0e-8, abs.(comparison.stata))
comparison.match = comparison.reldiff .<= 1e-4
comparison
```

Read the table as follows.

* **Every coefficient, every $R^2$, the deviance, both log pseudo-likelihoods
  and Wooldridge's $\hat\sigma$ match the published Stata output** to at least
  six significant digits.
* **The two robust standard-error rows now match too**, at the $10^{-4}$
  relative tolerance: `educ SE` in Example 19.1 is $0.002591756$ vs Stata's
  $0.0025918$ and `_cons SE` is $0.1477633$ in both; Example 19.2 behaves the
  same way. `StatEcon`'s `stata_glm` and Stata's `glm ..., vce(robust)` scale
  the sandwich by the same $n/(n-1)$ small-sample factor, so every $z$
  statistic, p-value and conclusion in both examples agrees.
